humanities_role_explanation: Uses narrative text passages from unpublished novels (Literature) to test and analyze long-range discourse understanding and linguistic constraints (Language & Linguistics).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only word prediction task using narrative passages of approximately 4.6 context sentences and a single target sentence.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Predicting the final word of a narrative passage, which operationalizes the model's capacity for tracking character referents, resolving pragmatic contexts, and conducting narrative-level causal inference over multiple sentences.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed to evaluate general text understanding and contextual language modeling capabilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates early neural language models such as LSTMs, simple RNNs, and Memory Networks, rather than modern transformer-based generative LLMs.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative language models are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Constructed from the Book Corpus, directly involving Literature and Literary Theory, and targeting Language and Linguistics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Models are evaluated using word prediction accuracy, perplexity, and the median rank of the correct target word.

published_after_march_2023.note: The paper was published in June 2016.

english_language.note: The paper and the underlying dataset are written entirely in English.

summary: The paper introduces the LAMBADA dataset, a collection of 10,022 narrative book passages designed to test language models on long-range discourse understanding. The task requires predicting a target word that is easily guessable by human subjects when provided with the full multi-sentence context, but impossible to guess from the final sentence alone. The authors demonstrate that contemporary language models fail to utilize this broad context, yielding near-zero accuracy on the benchmark.
key_findings: All evaluated neural and statistical language models (including LSTM, RNN, and Memory Networks) failed to achieve even 1% accuracy on the LAMBADA dataset, with the best model (N-Gram w/cache) achieving only 0.1% accuracy. This stands in stark contrast to their strong performance on an unfiltered control dataset (up to 21.9% accuracy) and a simple heuristic of guessing a capitalized word from the passage which achieved 7.3% accuracy.
